title: Poppy seed cake
subtitle: A dense, moist loaf cake with lemon and lightly ground poppy seeds
excerpt: Lightly grinding the poppy seeds before adding them to the batter releases their oils and gives a more pronounced, nutty flavour than adding them whole. Lemon zest and a post-bake lemon glaze keep the cake sharp and bright. This is a loaf-tin cake with a tight, moist crumb that keeps well for three days. Preparation takes about 25 minutes.
sourceNotes: Poppy seed cakes are central to Central and Eastern European baking, particularly in Polish makowiec, Czech makovy kolac and Hungarian pozsonyi kifli. The lemon and poppy seed combination became widespread in British baking in the 1990s via American influence. The method here is closest to the British drizzle-style loaf cake popularised by Nigella Lawson in 'How to Eat' (1998).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the glaze for up to 2 months. Glaze after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps for 3 days at room temperature, wrapped. The crumb softens and the lemon flavour deepens on the second day.

glossaryTerms.definition: Whole poppy seeds contribute texture to a cake batter but relatively little flavour, because the aromatic oils are locked inside their hard seed coats. Grinding the seeds briefly in a spice grinder or blender, to a rough meal rather than a fine powder, releases those oils and gives a more pronounced, nutty poppy seed flavour. The ground seeds also distribute more evenly through the batter than whole seeds, which tend to cluster. Partial grinding, leaving some seeds whole, gives both flavour and texture.

content.text: The distinctive blue-grey flecks in this cake come from the poppy seeds, but the flavour comes from

. Pulse them in a spice grinder for 5 to 10 seconds, stopping while some seeds are still whole. If you do not have a grinder, bruise them briefly with a pestle and mortar; whole seeds still improve the texture even if the flavour gain is smaller.
The lemon drizzle is applied while the cake is still warm and in the tin, so it soaks into the crumb rather than sitting as a glaze on top. Use all of it: the sharpness is what lifts the cake.

items.prepNote: lightly ground in a spice grinder
zest of both, juice of both (about 80 ml)

content.text: Heat the oven to 170 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin. Beat the 150 g butter and 175 g caster sugar until pale and fluffy, about 3 minutes. Beat in the 3 eggs one at a time. Stir in the lemon zest. Combine the 200 g flour, 1.5 tsp baking powder and ground poppy seeds, then fold into the batter alternately with the 60 ml milk.

Pour into the tin and bake for 45 to 55 minutes. The top should be golden brown with a slight crack down the centre, the edges will pull from the tin, and a skewer in the centre should come out clean.

While the cake bakes, stir the 80 g caster sugar into the 80 ml lemon juice. As soon as the cake comes out of the oven, prick all over with a skewer. Spoon the lemon mixture over the surface slowly, one spoonful at a time, allowing each to soak in. Leave in the tin until completely cool.

items.problem: Poppy seed flavour is very faint
items.solution: The seeds were added whole rather than ground. Even a brief pulse in a spice grinder, 5 to 10 seconds, makes a significant difference to the flavour. Alternatively, use slightly more seeds (up to 90 g) if grinding is not possible.

items.problem: Drizzle pools on top rather than soaking in
items.solution: Either the holes were too shallow, or the cake had cooled. Drizzle while the cake is hot. Push the skewer all the way to the tin base.

items.problem: Dense, wet crumb despite correct bake time
items.solution: The batter was overloaded with poppy seeds. Stick to 70 g maximum; more than this and the seeds absorb moisture from the crumb and make it heavy. Grind them before adding to avoid absorption issues.
